title: The Twenty-fifth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
November 19, 2023 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Twenty-fifth Sunday after Pentecost, falling in Ordinary Time of the Christian calendar, approximately four weeks before Advent begins on December 3, 2023. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would likely include Matthew 25:31-46 (the Parable of the Sheep and the Goats), which focuses on eschatological judgment and caring for "the least of these." This passage would be particularly relevant given its themes of stewardship and social responsibility, fitting the late autumn period of spiritual preparation before the new church year.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of its 80th General Convention held in Louisville, Kentucky in July 2023, where significant decisions on LGBTQ+ inclusion and prayer book revision were made. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kimberly Lucas (elected 2022), was navigating post-Convention implementation in parishes. Saint John's Cathedral specifically, as the mother church of the diocese, would have been engaged in diocesan initiatives around this transition period, though no extraordinary diocesan events are documented for this specific week.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced unseasonably warm weather in mid-November 2023, with temperatures in the 60s Fahrenheit before a cold front was expected mid-week. Local news focused on preparations for the holiday season and holiday shopping events beginning after Thanksgiving (November 23 that year). The Colorado legislature was in recess, with attention turning toward the 2024 election cycle and local ballot measures from the November 2023 municipal elections held in certain Colorado jurisdictions.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was consumed by a major United Auto Workers (UAW) strike that had begun in late September, with contract negotiations still ongoing—this represented one of the year's most significant labor actions. Former President Donald Trump was facing multiple indictments and was actively campaigning for the 2024 Republican nomination; the first Republican primary debates had already occurred. The House of Representatives remained in significant turmoil following Speaker Kevin McCarthy's ouster in October, with a new speaker not yet confirmed, creating legislative gridlock heading into the critical lame-duck session.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Israel-Hamas war had begun with Hamas's October 7 attack and Israel's subsequent invasion of Gaza, creating a major international crisis and humanitarian emergency with deep divisions emerging in American public discourse and religious communities. Ukraine continued its counteroffensive against Russian invasion forces, with winter warfare conditions becoming increasingly difficult. The COP28 climate negotiations were scheduled to begin in Dubai on November 30, with early positioning by nations on carbon transition pathways generating significant diplomatic attention.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture was in a transitional moment between Halloween and the Christmas season, with the Thanksgiving holiday (November 23) marking a psychological shift toward year-end reflection and gratitude, though commercial pressure for holiday spending was intensifying. There was heightened social anxiety about AI, with ChatGPT's one-year anniversary approaching and ongoing debates about artificial intelligence's implications for labor and society. Sports culture was focused on college and professional football playoffs; the Denver Broncos were struggling at mid-season (5-5 record), while college football's playoff race was tightening—the general mood combined cautious hope for holiday traditions with underlying concern about geopolitical instability, economic uncertainty, and technological disruption.
